prompt: In John 14, where did Jesus say there are many homes?
choices_json: In my Father's house
In the temple courts
In the city of David
In the wilderness
correct_answer: In my Father's house
explanation: Jesus says, 'In my Father's house are many homes' (John 14:2).

prompt: Which disciple asked, 'How can we know the way?' in John 14?
choices_json: Thomas
Philip
Judas (not Iscariot)
Peter
correct_answer: Thomas
explanation: Thomas asks this when Jesus speaks about where he is going (John 14:5).

prompt: In John 14:6, which description did Jesus give of himself?
choices_json: The way, the truth, and the life
The door, the shepherd, and the bread
The vine, the farmer, and the branches
The prophet, the king, and the judge
correct_answer: The way, the truth, and the life
explanation: Jesus says, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' (John 14:6).

prompt: Which disciple said, 'Lord, show us the Father' in John 14?
choices_json: Philip
Thomas
Judas (not Iscariot)
Andrew
correct_answer: Philip
explanation: Philip asks Jesus to show them the Father (John 14:8).

prompt: If the disciples loved Jesus, what did he tell them to keep?
choices_json: My commandments
My peace
My name
My house
correct_answer: My commandments
explanation: Jesus says, 'If you love me, keep my commandments' (John 14:15).

prompt: How long did Jesus say the Counselor would be with the disciples?
choices_json: Forever
For a little while
Until morning
For three days
correct_answer: Forever
explanation: Jesus says the Father will give another Counselor 'that he may be with you forever' (John 14:16).

prompt: What did Jesus say he was leaving with his disciples in John 14:27?
choices_json: Peace
A sign
A throne
A scroll
correct_answer: Peace
explanation: Jesus tells them, 'Peace I leave with you' (John 14:27).

prompt: Why did Jesus say he was going ahead in John 14:2?
choices_json: To prepare a place for you
To gather the crowds
To cleanse the temple again
To hide from the rulers
correct_answer: To prepare a place for you
explanation: Jesus says he is going 'to prepare a place for you' (John 14:2).

prompt: What did Jesus say he would do after preparing a place for his disciples?
choices_json: Come again and receive you to myself
Send them back to Galilee
Build them a temple
Call angels to escort them
correct_answer: Come again and receive you to myself
explanation: He promises, 'I will come again, and will receive you to myself' (John 14:3).

prompt: If the disciples struggled to believe Jesus' words in John 14:11, what did he tell them to consider?
choices_json: The very works themselves
The opinion of the crowds
The memory of Moses
The power of Rome
correct_answer: The very works themselves
explanation: Jesus says to believe 'for the very works' sake' (John 14:11).

prompt: Why did Jesus say believers would do greater works than his in John 14:12?
choices_json: Because I am going to my Father
Because the temple would fall
Because the crowds would grow
Because the Law was ending
correct_answer: Because I am going to my Father
explanation: Jesus ties the greater works to his going to the Father (John 14:12).

prompt: Why does Jesus say the world cannot receive the Spirit of truth?
choices_json: Because it doesn't see him and doesn't know him
Because it has no prophets left
Because it already has the Law
Because it lives too far away
correct_answer: Because it doesn't see him and doesn't know him
explanation: Jesus says the world cannot receive the Spirit 'for it doesn't see him and doesn't know him' (John 14:17).

prompt: In John 14:18, Jesus said, 'I will not leave you as' what?
choices_json: Orphans
Servants
Strangers
Captives
correct_answer: Orphans
explanation: Jesus promises, 'I will not leave you orphans' (John 14:18).

prompt: According to John 14:26, what will the Holy Spirit do for the disciples?
choices_json: Teach them all things and remind them of Jesus' words
Lead them into the wilderness and test them
Build many homes in the Father's house
Keep the prince of the world from coming
correct_answer: Teach them all things and remind them of Jesus' words
explanation: Jesus says the Holy Spirit will teach them and remind them of what he said (John 14:26).

prompt: When Philip asked to see the Father, what evidence did Jesus point to alongside his words?
choices_json: The Father's works being done in him
The disciples' future courage
The city's welcome of him
The priests' approval of him
correct_answer: The Father's works being done in him
explanation: Jesus says the Father living in him does his works (John 14:10-11).

prompt: In John 14, why is asking in Jesus' name tied to answered prayer?
choices_json: So that the Father may be glorified in the Son
So that the disciples may impress the crowds
So that the rulers may fear the signs
So that no one will ever question their authority or message again
correct_answer: So that the Father may be glorified in the Son
explanation: Jesus says he will do what is asked in his name 'that the Father may be glorified in the Son' (John 14:13).

prompt: Why does Judas (not Iscariot) sound surprised in John 14:22?
choices_json: Jesus said he would reveal himself to the disciples and not to the world
Jesus said he would leave without speaking again
Jesus said the Father would stop loving the world
Jesus said the temple would become their home
correct_answer: Jesus said he would reveal himself to the disciples and not to the world
explanation: Judas asks why Jesus is about to reveal himself to them and not to the world (John 14:22).

prompt: In John 14:19, what is the disciples' future life connected to?
choices_json: Jesus' own continuing life
Their success in greater works
Their courage before the world
Their place among Israel's teachers
correct_answer: Jesus' own continuing life
explanation: Jesus says, 'Because I live, you will live also' (John 14:19).

prompt: What repeated pattern in John 14 shows that love for Jesus is more than words?
choices_json: Keeping his commandments and his word
Traveling wherever he travels
Defending him before the Pharisees
Building homes for one another
correct_answer: Keeping his commandments and his word
explanation: Jesus repeatedly links love for him with keeping his commandments and word (John 14:15, 21, 23-24).

prompt: Why did Jesus tell the disciples these things before they happened?
choices_json: So that when it happened, they might believe
So that they could argue with the rulers
So that they would return to Bethany
So that they would appoint new leaders
correct_answer: So that when it happened, they might believe
explanation: Jesus says he told them beforehand so that 'when it happens, you may believe' (John 14:29).

prompt: What does Jesus imply by saying the prince of the world 'has nothing in me'?
choices_json: He has no claim on Jesus
He will soon become Jesus' ally
He controls the disciples for a season
He is stronger than the Father
correct_answer: He has no claim on Jesus
explanation: Jesus says the prince of the world comes, but 'he has nothing in me' (John 14:30).

prompt: In John 14:6, how does Jesus define the disciples' route to the Father?
choices_json: Jesus himself is the only way to the Father
The Law and the temple together open the way
The disciples' obedience creates a path on its own
Many spiritual roads lead to the same Father
correct_answer: Jesus himself is the only way to the Father
explanation: Jesus says, 'No one comes to the Father, except through me' (John 14:6).

prompt: When Jesus says, 'He who has seen me has seen the Father,' what claim is he making in John 14?
choices_json: He uniquely reveals the Father in his own person
He is simply repeating the prophets' message about God to Israel
He is saying the Father has no distinct will
He is claiming to replace every earlier sign
correct_answer: He uniquely reveals the Father in his own person
explanation: Jesus tells Philip that seeing him is seeing the Father (John 14:9).

prompt: What does the promise of 'another Counselor' suggest in the flow of John 14?
choices_json: Jesus' help will continue through the Spirit after he goes
The disciples will no longer need Jesus' help or presence at all
A new earthly ruler will defend the group
The world will finally begin to understand him
correct_answer: Jesus' help will continue through the Spirit after he goes
explanation: The Father gives 'another Counselor' as Jesus prepares to depart (John 14:16-18).

prompt: In John 14, how are love and obedience related?
choices_json: Obedience is the lived expression of love for Jesus
Obedience replaces any need for love
Love matters only after the kingdom arrives
Love and obedience belong to separate stages of faith
correct_answer: Obedience is the lived expression of love for Jesus
explanation: Jesus ties loving him to keeping his commandments and word (John 14:15, 21, 23).

prompt: Why is Jesus' peace different from the peace the world gives in John 14?
choices_json: It steadies troubled hearts while he speaks of departure and conflict
It depends on political stability in Jerusalem
It comes only after the disciples prove themselves faithful under pressure
It disappears whenever the prince of the world appears
correct_answer: It steadies troubled hearts while he speaks of departure and conflict
explanation: Jesus gives peace that answers troubled and fearful hearts, unlike the world's kind (John 14:27).

prompt: How does 'we will come to him and make our home with him' deepen the chapter's comfort?
choices_json: God's presence comes to obedient believers even before the final dwelling
Only a few disciples will receive a private house in Jerusalem
The promise of heaven is postponed until the temple is rebuilt
The disciples are told to construct a lasting home for God by their own effort
correct_answer: God's presence comes to obedient believers even before the final dwelling
explanation: Jesus says the Father and Son will make their home with the one who loves him (John 14:23).

prompt: When Jesus says the disciples should rejoice because he is going to the Father, what perspective is he teaching?
choices_json: His departure should be viewed through the Father's glory rather than only their loss
Their grief proves they have not followed him at all
His return to the Father means the mission on earth has failed before the disciples were ready
The disciples should stop expecting Jesus to come to them again
correct_answer: His departure should be viewed through the Father's glory rather than only their loss
explanation: Jesus says they should rejoice because he is going to the Father (John 14:28).

prompt: How does Jesus' promise to prepare a place and receive the disciples to himself frame their future in John 14?
choices_json: Their hope is being with Jesus where he is
Their hope is mainly gaining honor before the world
Their hope is escaping all responsibility in the present
Their hope is ruling the temple before Jesus returns
correct_answer: Their hope is being with Jesus where he is
explanation: Jesus prepares a place so that 'where I am, you may be there also' (John 14:2-3).

prompt: In John 14, why do Jesus' works matter when belief feels difficult?
choices_json: They display the Father's activity in the Son
They replace the need for Jesus' words entirely
They prove the crowds already understand him
They guarantee the world will receive the Spirit
correct_answer: They display the Father's activity in the Son
explanation: Jesus points to the works because the Father dwelling in him does them (John 14:10-11).

prompt: What is the theological weight of 'I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you' in John 14:20?
choices_json: It joins Jesus' union with the Father to the believers' union with Jesus
It says the disciples become the Father in exactly the same sense as Jesus himself
It means the disciples no longer need the Holy Spirit
It limits fellowship with Jesus to the eleven alone
correct_answer: It joins Jesus' union with the Father to the believers' union with Jesus
explanation: Jesus links his relation to the Father with the disciples' life in him (John 14:20).

prompt: How does John 14:26 support the trustworthiness of the disciples' later witness?
choices_json: The Holy Spirit will teach them and remind them of Jesus' words
Their memories alone will be enough once Jesus is gone
Only Thomas and Philip will understand what happened
The prince of the world will force the truth into the open for every nation
correct_answer: The Holy Spirit will teach them and remind them of Jesus' words
explanation: Jesus says the Spirit will teach and remind them of all he said (John 14:26).

prompt: What does 'the prince of the world comes' followed by 'he has nothing in me' imply about the coming conflict?
choices_json: Jesus faces it without any rightful claim of evil against him
Jesus expects to compromise with the ruler of this world
The disciples will defeat the prince before Jesus suffers
The Father has temporarily surrendered authority to the world
correct_answer: Jesus faces it without any rightful claim of evil against him
explanation: Jesus says the prince of the world has nothing in him as the conflict approaches (John 14:30).

prompt: In John 14:31, what will the world learn from Jesus doing exactly what the Father commanded?
choices_json: That Jesus loves the Father
That Jesus fears the prince of the world
That Jesus no longer intends to return
That Jesus must persuade the Father to accept him
correct_answer: That Jesus loves the Father
explanation: Jesus says he acts as the Father commanded 'that the world may know that I love the Father' (John 14:31).

prompt: How does John 14 hold together future place and present presence?
choices_json: Jesus prepares a place ahead while the Father and Son make their home with obedient believers now
The chapter cares only about heaven and not about present fellowship
The chapter replaces future hope with inward feelings alone
The chapter teaches that God's presence is limited to the upper room and nowhere else
correct_answer: Jesus prepares a place ahead while the Father and Son make their home with obedient believers now
explanation: The chapter speaks of a prepared place and of the Father and Son making their home with the believer (John 14:2-3, 23).

prompt: In John 15, what did Jesus call himself?
choices_json: The true vine
The open gate
The bright star
The living altar
correct_answer: The true vine
explanation: Jesus says, 'I am the true vine' (John 15:1).

prompt: Who did Jesus say is the farmer in John 15?
choices_json: The Father
Moses
The Counselor
The beloved disciple
correct_answer: The Father
explanation: Jesus says, 'My Father is the farmer' (John 15:1).

prompt: In the vine picture, what did Jesus call the disciples?
choices_json: Branches
Servants
Seeds
Laborers
correct_answer: Branches
explanation: Jesus tells them, 'You are the branches' (John 15:5).

prompt: What does the Father do to a branch that bears fruit?
choices_json: He prunes it
He burns it
He hides it
He uproots it
correct_answer: He prunes it
explanation: Jesus says every fruitful branch is pruned so it may bear more fruit (John 15:2).

prompt: According to John 15:5, apart from Jesus the disciples can do what?
choices_json: Nothing
Very little
Only endure hatred
Only ask for help
correct_answer: Nothing
explanation: Jesus says, 'Apart from me you can do nothing' (John 15:5).

prompt: What did Jesus call the disciples instead of servants in John 15?
choices_json: Friends
Guests
Watchmen
Sowers
correct_answer: Friends
explanation: Jesus says, 'No longer do I call you servants ... I have called you friends' (John 15:15).

prompt: What command did Jesus repeat in John 15:12?
choices_json: Love one another as I have loved you
Guard the temple as I guarded it
Wait in Jerusalem until I return
Judge the crowds with righteous judgment
correct_answer: Love one another as I have loved you
explanation: Jesus says, 'This is my commandment, that you love one another, even as I have loved you' (John 15:12).

prompt: Why did Jesus say the disciples were already clean in John 15:3?
choices_json: Because of the word he had spoken to them
Because they had left their nets
Because they had washed in the Jordan
Because they had kept every command perfectly
correct_answer: Because of the word he had spoken to them
explanation: Jesus says, 'You are already pruned clean because of the word which I have spoken to you' (John 15:3).

prompt: What happens to a branch that does not remain in the vine, according to John 15:6?
choices_json: It is thrown out, withers, and is burned
It is replanted beside the vine and watered
It grows fruit on its own after a delay
It becomes the farmer's tool for pruning
correct_answer: It is thrown out, withers, and is burned
explanation: Jesus says the branch is thrown out, withers, and is cast into the fire (John 15:6).

prompt: If Jesus' words remain in the disciples, what did he say they may do?
choices_json: Ask whatever they desire
Judge every nation
Call down fire from heaven
Hide from the hatred of the world
correct_answer: Ask whatever they desire
explanation: Jesus says, 'You will ask whatever you desire, and it will be done for you' (John 15:7).

prompt: According to John 15:8, what glorifies the Father?
choices_json: Bearing much fruit
Leaving the world quietly
Keeping sorrow to themselves
Winning arguments with persecutors
correct_answer: Bearing much fruit
explanation: Jesus says the Father is glorified when the disciples bear much fruit (John 15:8).

prompt: What is the greatest love described in John 15:13?
choices_json: That someone lay down his life for his friends
That someone share his harvest with strangers
That someone defend his teacher in public
That someone remain silent under pressure
correct_answer: That someone lay down his life for his friends
explanation: Jesus says, 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends' (John 15:13).

prompt: Why did Jesus say he called the disciples friends?
choices_json: Because he made known to them what he heard from the Father
Because they had already borne more fruit than anyone
Because the world had begun to admire them
Because they no longer needed any commands
correct_answer: Because he made known to them what he heard from the Father
explanation: Jesus says he called them friends because he had made known what he heard from the Father (John 15:15).

prompt: What saying did Jesus tell the disciples to remember when persecution came?
choices_json: A servant is not greater than his lord
Whoever seeks finds
Peace I leave with you
Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it
correct_answer: A servant is not greater than his lord
explanation: Jesus says, 'Remember the word that I said to you: "A servant is not greater than his lord"' (John 15:20).

prompt: How does remaining in Jesus affect fruit in the vine passage?
choices_json: It is the only way branches bear much fruit
It guarantees the world will love the disciples
It replaces any need for prayer
It keeps every branch from ever being pruned
correct_answer: It is the only way branches bear much fruit
explanation: Jesus says a branch cannot bear fruit by itself unless it remains in the vine (John 15:4-5).

prompt: Why is pruning not a sign of abandonment in John 15?
choices_json: It is aimed at producing more fruit
It proves the branch was never connected
It shows the farmer has stopped caring
It means the season of fruit is over
correct_answer: It is aimed at producing more fruit
explanation: Jesus says the Father prunes fruitful branches 'that it may bear more fruit' (John 15:2).

prompt: What link does John 15 make between obedience and joy?
choices_json: Keeping Jesus' commandments leads to remaining in his love and to full joy
Keeping Jesus' commandments leads mainly to duty now, with joy belonging only to the next age
Joy is found by escaping commands and pruning
Obedience produces fear rather than love
correct_answer: Keeping Jesus' commandments leads to remaining in his love and to full joy
explanation: Jesus connects keeping his commandments, remaining in his love, and joy being made full (John 15:10-11).

prompt: How does Jesus' choice of the disciples shape the way they should view their mission in John 15?
choices_json: He chose and appointed them to go and bear fruit
They created their own mission by deciding to follow first
Their task is mainly to avoid the world until he returns
Only their private devotion matters, not any outward fruit
correct_answer: He chose and appointed them to go and bear fruit
explanation: Jesus says, 'I chose you and appointed you, that you should go and bear fruit' (John 15:16).

prompt: Why does Jesus connect loving one another with the world's hatred in the same chapter?
choices_json: Internal love is meant to sustain the disciples amid external opposition
Love for one another is how they avoid persecution altogether from the hostile world
The world will hate them only if they fail to love one another
Hatred from the world is simply another form of pruning
correct_answer: Internal love is meant to sustain the disciples amid external opposition
explanation: Jesus commands mutual love and then prepares them for the world's hatred (John 15:12, 17-19).

prompt: In John 15, what does the world's hatred reveal about its relation to Jesus?
choices_json: It hated him first and does not know the one who sent him
It secretly belongs to him even while resisting
It only objects to the disciples and not to Jesus himself
It hates him because his disciples have withdrawn from society
correct_answer: It hated him first and does not know the one who sent him
explanation: Jesus says the world hated him before it hated the disciples and does not know the one who sent him (John 15:18, 21).

prompt: Why are the disciples' testimony and the Counselor's testimony paired at the end of John 15?
choices_json: The Spirit testifies about Jesus, and they testify as those who have been with him from the beginning
The disciples replace the Spirit once Jesus departs
The Spirit speaks only to the world while the disciples speak only to one another about Jesus
The testimony belongs only to future believers, not to the eleven
correct_answer: The Spirit testifies about Jesus, and they testify as those who have been with him from the beginning
explanation: Jesus says the Counselor will testify about him, and the disciples will also testify because they have been with him from the beginning (John 15:26-27).

prompt: By calling himself the 'true vine' before speaking of branches and fruit, what claim does Jesus make in John 15?
choices_json: He is the genuine source of the fruitfulness God's people need
He is only one helpful source among many others
He is abandoning Israel's story in favor of a new religion
He is teaching mainly about agriculture rather than discipleship
correct_answer: He is the genuine source of the fruitfulness God's people need
explanation: Jesus presents himself as the true vine from whom fruitfulness actually comes (John 15:1-5).

prompt: How does 'apart from me you can do nothing' define discipleship in John 15?
choices_json: It is a life of dependence on Jesus rather than self-generated strength
It means disciples should stop attempting any good work until they feel spiritually stronger
It limits obedience to moments of visible miracles
It says only apostles can bear fruit
correct_answer: It is a life of dependence on Jesus rather than self-generated strength
explanation: Jesus says fruit-bearing is impossible apart from remaining in him (John 15:4-5).

prompt: Why does Jesus move from servants to friends before speaking of laying down his life?
choices_json: He shows that shared knowledge and personal love shape the community he is about to die for
He wants the disciples to forget every command he has given them
He is explaining that friendship removes every difference in authority and command
He is preparing to hand the mission over to the world
correct_answer: He shows that shared knowledge and personal love shape the community he is about to die for
explanation: Jesus calls them friends because he has made known the Father's words, then speaks of laying down his life for friends (John 15:13-15).

prompt: How does John 15:13 prepare the reader for the cross?
choices_json: It frames Jesus' death as the greatest act of love for his friends
It treats Jesus' death as an unfortunate loss of influence and nothing more
It says the cross will matter only as an example of courage
It shifts attention away from Jesus onto the disciples' sacrifice
correct_answer: It frames Jesus' death as the greatest act of love for his friends
explanation: The saying about laying down one's life gives Jesus' coming death the shape of supreme love (John 15:13).

prompt: What does 'your fruit should remain' add to the mission Jesus gives in John 15:16?
choices_json: Jesus appoints them for lasting fruit, not momentary results
Fruit matters only at the final judgment, not in present mission
The disciples are told to preserve literal vineyard harvests
Remaining fruit means never facing hatred from the world
correct_answer: Jesus appoints them for lasting fruit, not momentary results
explanation: Jesus says he appointed them to bear fruit 'and that your fruit should remain' (John 15:16).

prompt: Why does Jesus say the world hates the disciples in John 15?
choices_json: Because they are not of the world and were chosen out of it
Because they have stopped speaking about him publicly
Because they are more powerful than Rome
Because they no longer need the Father's love
correct_answer: Because they are not of the world and were chosen out of it
explanation: Jesus says the world hates them because he chose them out of the world (John 15:19).

prompt: How do Jesus' words and works remove excuses in John 15:22-24?
choices_json: They bring such clear revelation that rejection becomes culpable
They guarantee everyone who hears will believe immediately and permanently
They cancel the need for any future testimony
They prove sin no longer exists after Jesus speaks
correct_answer: They bring such clear revelation that rejection becomes culpable
explanation: Jesus says those who heard his words and saw his works now have 'no excuse for their sin' (John 15:22-24).

prompt: How does John 15 connect the Father's pruning and the Son's abiding care?
choices_json: The Father cultivates while life and fruit flow only through remaining in the Son
The Father and the Son take opposite roles in the disciples' growth
The Father prunes only after disciples learn to live apart from Jesus
The Son bears fruit for the disciples so their response and remaining no longer matter at all
correct_answer: The Father cultivates while life and fruit flow only through remaining in the Son
explanation: The Father prunes, but branches bear fruit only by remaining in Jesus (John 15:2, 4-5).

prompt: In John 15, what is the significance of remaining in Jesus' love 'even as' he remains in the Father's love?
choices_json: Discipleship mirrors the Son's own obedient relationship with the Father
Jesus is saying the disciples now replace him in the Father's love and favor
The Father's love and Jesus' love are unrelated realities
Only one disciple will be able to remain in that love
correct_answer: Discipleship mirrors the Son's own obedient relationship with the Father
explanation: Jesus compares the disciples' remaining in his love to his own obedience within the Father's love (John 15:9-10).

prompt: Why is the command to love one another central rather than optional in the vine discourse?
choices_json: Fruit, friendship, and witness are all shaped by Christlike love
It is included mainly to soften the hard warning about burned branches
It matters only when the world begins to like the disciples
It replaces the need to remain in Jesus himself
correct_answer: Fruit, friendship, and witness are all shaped by Christlike love
explanation: Jesus anchors the chapter's community life in the repeated command to love one another (John 15:12, 17).

prompt: What does Jesus' statement 'You didn't choose me, but I chose you' contribute to John 15's view of discipleship?
choices_json: Initiative and mission begin with Jesus' call
The disciples have no responsibility once they are chosen
Choice belongs only to the world, not to Jesus
The chapter denies that the disciples actually followed him
correct_answer: Initiative and mission begin with Jesus' call
explanation: Jesus grounds their mission in his choosing and appointing of them (John 15:16).

prompt: How does the quotation 'They hated me without a cause' function in John 15?
choices_json: It shows Jesus' rejection fulfills Scripture rather than disproving his mission
It proves the disciples will never be hated in the same way
It means hatred comes only from people outside Israel
It turns hatred into an excuse for retaliating in kind
correct_answer: It shows Jesus' rejection fulfills Scripture rather than disproving his mission
explanation: Jesus says their hostility fulfills what was written in their law: 'They hated me without a cause' (John 15:25).

prompt: What does the Counselor's coming 'from the Father' and testifying 'about me' show at the end of John 15?
choices_json: The Spirit's ministry is both Father-sent and centered on Jesus
The Spirit will speak independently of both Father and Son
The Spirit's only task is comforting disciples in private moments of fear
The Spirit replaces the disciples' witness altogether
correct_answer: The Spirit's ministry is both Father-sent and centered on Jesus
explanation: Jesus says he will send the Counselor from the Father, and that Counselor will testify about him (John 15:26).

prompt: How does John 15 hold together suffering and mission?
choices_json: The same disciples who are hated by the world are appointed to bear lasting fruit and testify
Mission ends whenever suffering begins
Fruitfulness is promised only in peaceful seasons
Hatred from the world means the disciples should remain silent
correct_answer: The same disciples who are hated by the world are appointed to bear lasting fruit and testify
explanation: Jesus appoints the disciples to bear fruit and then prepares them for hatred while calling them to testify (John 15:16, 18-19, 27).

prompt: In John 16, from what places did Jesus say the disciples would be put out?
choices_json: The synagogues
The marketplaces
The temple treasury
The upper rooms
correct_answer: The synagogues
explanation: Jesus says, 'They will put you out of the synagogues' (John 16:2).

prompt: What did Jesus say had filled the disciples' hearts in John 16:6?
choices_json: Sorrow
Joy
Courage
Anger
correct_answer: Sorrow
explanation: Jesus says, 'Sorrow has filled your heart' (John 16:6).

prompt: Who would come to the disciples if Jesus went away?
choices_json: The Counselor
John the Baptist
Elijah
An angel with a sword
correct_answer: The Counselor
explanation: Jesus says if he goes away, he will send the Counselor to them (John 16:7).

prompt: What three things will the Counselor convict the world about?
choices_json: Sin, righteousness, and judgment
Bread, water, and light
Faith, hope, and love
Priests, rulers, and crowds
correct_answer: Sin, righteousness, and judgment
explanation: Jesus says the Counselor will convict the world 'about sin, about righteousness, and about judgment' (John 16:8).

prompt: What everyday event did Jesus use to picture sorrow turning to joy in John 16?
choices_json: A woman giving birth
A farmer gathering grapes
A child learning to walk
A shepherd finding a lamb
correct_answer: A woman giving birth
explanation: Jesus compares their sorrow and joy to a woman in labor whose child is then born (John 16:21).

prompt: From whom did Jesus say he came into the world?
choices_json: The Father
The synagogue leaders
The house of David
The Spirit only
correct_answer: The Father
explanation: Jesus says, 'I came from the Father, and have come into the world' (John 16:28).

prompt: What did Jesus say he had overcome in John 16:33?
choices_json: The world
The grave
The empire
The night
correct_answer: The world
explanation: Jesus says, 'I have overcome the world' (John 16:33).

prompt: Why would some people think killing the disciples was service to God, according to John 16?
choices_json: Because they had not known the Father or Jesus
Because they misunderstood Moses' signs
Because the disciples would hide in fear
Because the Spirit would stop speaking to them
correct_answer: Because they had not known the Father or Jesus
explanation: Jesus says such people act this way because they have not known the Father nor him (John 16:2-3).

prompt: Why did Jesus say he had not told the disciples these things from the beginning?
choices_json: Because he was with them
Because they had already understood them
Because the rulers were listening nearby
Because the time of sorrow had not yet come
correct_answer: Because he was with them
explanation: Jesus says, 'I didn't tell you these things from the beginning, because I was with you' (John 16:4).

prompt: According to John 16:9, why will the Spirit convict the world concerning sin?
choices_json: Because they don't believe in Jesus
Because they don't keep the feast days
Because they neglect the poor
Because they fail to recognize the disciples
correct_answer: Because they don't believe in Jesus
explanation: Jesus says the Spirit convicts 'about sin, because they don't believe in me' (John 16:9).

prompt: According to John 16:11, why will the Spirit convict the world concerning judgment?
choices_json: Because the prince of this world has been judged
Because the disciples will judge the nations
Because Jerusalem is about to fall
Because the synagogue rulers have lost their place
correct_answer: Because the prince of this world has been judged
explanation: Jesus says the Spirit convicts 'about judgment, because the prince of this world has been judged' (John 16:11).

prompt: What will the Spirit of truth speak, according to John 16:13?
choices_json: Whatever he hears
Only figures of speech
Only what the disciples first decide
Whatever the world wants to hear
correct_answer: Whatever he hears
explanation: Jesus says the Spirit 'will not speak from himself; but whatever he hears, he will speak' (John 16:13).

prompt: Until John 16:24, what had the disciples asked nothing in?
choices_json: Jesus' name
The temple courts
The Father's house
The world to come
correct_answer: Jesus' name
explanation: Jesus says, 'Until now, you have asked nothing in my name' (John 16:24).

prompt: How did the disciples describe Jesus' words in John 16:29?
choices_json: Speaking plainly and using no figures of speech
Hard to understand and deeply troubling
Like Moses speaking from the mountain
So quiet that no one could answer
correct_answer: Speaking plainly and using no figures of speech
explanation: The disciples say Jesus is now speaking plainly and not using figures of speech (John 16:29).

prompt: Why does Jesus say his departure is actually to the disciples' advantage in John 16?
choices_json: Because the Counselor will come to them
Because the rulers will stop opposing them
Because they will no longer have any sorrow
Because the world will finally receive him
correct_answer: Because the Counselor will come to them
explanation: Jesus says it is to their advantage that he goes away, since then the Counselor will come (John 16:7).

prompt: When the disciples repeat 'a little while' in John 16, what does their confusion show?
choices_json: They do not yet grasp Jesus' departure and return
They are eager to leave Jesus behind
They fully understand the resurrection already
They are arguing about which synagogue to enter next
correct_answer: They do not yet grasp Jesus' departure and return
explanation: The disciples admit, 'We don't know what he is saying' about 'a little while' (John 16:17-18).

prompt: How does the childbirth image interpret the disciples' coming grief in John 16?
choices_json: It will be intense but temporary and will give way to joy
It will slowly fade into forgetfulness without any clear cause
It proves sorrow is the final state of discipleship
It means only women can understand Jesus' departure
correct_answer: It will be intense but temporary and will give way to joy
explanation: Jesus compares their sorrow to labor pain that turns to joy once the child is born (John 16:20-21).

prompt: Why does Jesus say the disciples' joy cannot be taken away?
choices_json: Because he will see them again
Because the world will stop hating them
Because they will no longer ask the Father anything
Because they will remain together without scattering
correct_answer: Because he will see them again
explanation: Jesus says, 'I will see you again, and your heart will rejoice, and no one will take your joy away from you' (John 16:22).

prompt: What change does Jesus describe from figures of speech to plain speech about the Father?
choices_json: Clearer understanding about the Father will follow
The disciples must abandon all earlier teaching
Only private revelations will matter from then on
The Spirit will stop reminding them of Jesus' words
correct_answer: Clearer understanding about the Father will follow
explanation: Jesus says a time is coming when he will tell them plainly about the Father (John 16:25).

prompt: How does the Father's love for the disciples relate to Jesus in John 16:27?
choices_json: They have loved Jesus and believed he came from God
It depends on how bravely they face the rulers
It is unrelated to what they believe about Jesus
It begins only after they ask enough questions
correct_answer: They have loved Jesus and believed he came from God
explanation: Jesus says the Father loves them because they loved him and believed he came from God (John 16:27).

prompt: What does Jesus expose by asking, 'Do you now believe?' in John 16:31?
choices_json: Their confession is real but still fragile before the scattering
Their faith is already perfect and complete
Their belief never had any truth in it at all
Their problem is only that they asked too many questions earlier
correct_answer: Their confession is real but still fragile before the scattering
explanation: Jesus challenges their confidence and immediately predicts they will be scattered (John 16:30-32).

prompt: How does John 16 portray persecution done 'for God'?
choices_json: As zeal that still fails to know the Father or the Son
As the highest form of faithful worship
As a misunderstanding that Jesus leaves uncorrected
As a sign that the world is close to believing
correct_answer: As zeal that still fails to know the Father or the Son
explanation: Jesus says persecutors will think they offer service to God, yet they do not know the Father or him (John 16:2-3).

prompt: Why is the Spirit's convicting work centered on sin, righteousness, and judgment in John 16?
choices_json: Each category is defined by humanity's response to Jesus and his vindication
They are simply the three favorite themes of the disciples
The Spirit avoids speaking about Jesus directly
Those topics matter only until Jesus reaches the Father and then disappear
correct_answer: Each category is defined by humanity's response to Jesus and his vindication
explanation: Jesus explains each category in direct relation to belief in him, his going to the Father, and the ruler's judgment (John 16:9-11).

prompt: What does 'He will glorify me' reveal about the Spirit's role in John 16?
choices_json: The Spirit directs attention to Jesus rather than away from him
The Spirit begins a separate mission that replaces Jesus
The Spirit's main work is to glorify the disciples before the world
The Spirit speaks only about future events and not about Christ
correct_answer: The Spirit directs attention to Jesus rather than away from him
explanation: Jesus says the Spirit will glorify him by taking what is his and declaring it (John 16:14).

prompt: How does 'All things that the Father has are mine' function in the Spirit discourse of John 16?
choices_json: It grounds the Spirit's message in the shared reality of Father and Son
It limits the Father's work to what the disciples can understand
It shows Jesus has stopped depending on the Father
It means the Spirit speaks only of the Father and never truly of Jesus himself
correct_answer: It grounds the Spirit's message in the shared reality of Father and Son
explanation: Jesus says the Spirit takes from what is his, and then explains that all the Father's things are his (John 16:14-15).

prompt: What access does Jesus open up when he says the disciples may ask the Father in his name?
choices_json: Direct, confident access to the Father through Jesus
Access only through the synagogue leaders
Access only after Jesus persuades the Father to listen
Access that depends on the disciples never feeling sorrow again
correct_answer: Direct, confident access to the Father through Jesus
explanation: Jesus says they may ask the Father in his name, and the Father himself loves them (John 16:23, 26-27).

prompt: How does John 16 turn sorrow into a theological theme instead of mere emotion?
choices_json: Sorrow becomes the doorway to resurrection joy
Sorrow is treated as a failure that disqualifies discipleship
Sorrow matters only for the women in the crowd
Sorrow is solved by escaping the world altogether
correct_answer: Sorrow becomes the doorway to resurrection joy
explanation: Jesus says their sorrow will be turned into joy, like labor pains ending in birth (John 16:20-22).

prompt: Why can Jesus promise peace in the same chapter where he predicts scattering?
choices_json: Peace rests in him and in the Father's presence, not in the disciples' steadiness
Peace comes because the disciples will not actually scatter
Peace depends on the world becoming friendly to them
Peace is limited to the hours before Jesus is arrested
correct_answer: Peace rests in him and in the Father's presence, not in the disciples' steadiness
explanation: Jesus predicts scattering yet says the Father is with him and that in him they may have peace (John 16:32-33).

prompt: What does the triad of sin, righteousness, and judgment say about the world's crisis in John 16?
choices_json: The world's deepest problem is unbelief, its verdict on Jesus is overturned, and its ruler already stands condemned
The world mainly needs better education, stronger rulers, clearer customs, and wiser public leadership
The Spirit's work is to condemn disciples before addressing anyone else
The three themes are unrelated topics gathered only for emphasis
correct_answer: The world's deepest problem is unbelief, its verdict on Jesus is overturned, and its ruler already stands condemned
explanation: Jesus defines sin by unbelief, righteousness by his return to the Father, and judgment by the ruler's condemnation (John 16:9-11).

prompt: How does John 16:13-15 prevent 'guiding into all truth' from being treated as independent revelation detached from Jesus?
choices_json: The Spirit speaks what he hears, takes what belongs to Jesus, and glorifies him
The Spirit begins a message that no longer depends on Jesus' words
The disciples will create the truth for each new situation largely on their own
The Spirit's truth is limited to predictions about distant events
correct_answer: The Spirit speaks what he hears, takes what belongs to Jesus, and glorifies him
explanation: Jesus says the Spirit will not speak from himself but will declare what he hears and what belongs to Jesus (John 16:13-15).

prompt: What mission arc is compressed into Jesus' words, 'I came from the Father ... I leave the world and go to the Father'?
choices_json: Incarnation, earthly mission, departure, and return to the Father
Birth in Bethlehem, years in Nazareth, and a later move to Jerusalem
The rise of Israel, the exile, and the rebuilding of the temple
The disciples' call, failure, and eventual martyrdom
correct_answer: Incarnation, earthly mission, departure, and return to the Father
explanation: Jesus sums up his origin and destination in one line about coming from and returning to the Father (John 16:28).

prompt: How does Jesus' prediction that the disciples will scatter deepen the statement 'I am not alone' in John 16?
choices_json: Human companions fail, but the Father's presence remains
Jesus means the Counselor has already replaced every disciple
The scattering is only apparent because they stay spiritually nearby
Being alone is unimportant once the disciples stop asking questions
correct_answer: Human companions fail, but the Father's presence remains
explanation: Jesus says they will leave him alone, 'yet I am not alone, because the Father is with me' (John 16:32).

prompt: What kind of joy does John 16 promise after the disciples' sorrow?
choices_json: Resurrection-grounded joy that no one can take away
A short burst of relief before deeper fear and confusion return again
Public honor from the same world that hated Jesus
Joy only for the disciples who never doubted
correct_answer: Resurrection-grounded joy that no one can take away
explanation: Jesus says he will see them again, their hearts will rejoice, and no one will take that joy away (John 16:22).

prompt: Why does Jesus challenge the disciples with 'Do you now believe?' right after their confident confession?
choices_json: To show that their understanding is about to be tested by crisis
To deny that they have believed anything at all
To teach that questions are always a sign of unbelief
To tell them belief matters less than steady courage when suffering arrives
correct_answer: To show that their understanding is about to be tested by crisis
explanation: Jesus questions their confidence and then predicts their immediate scattering (John 16:30-32).

prompt: How does the closing line 'I have overcome the world' function before the arrest actually happens?
choices_json: It announces victory in advance, so the coming cross is faced as triumph rather than defeat
It means the disciples will be spared every form of trouble
It signals that Jesus no longer expects resistance from the world
It refers only to a future age and not to the present crisis already beginning around them
correct_answer: It announces victory in advance, so the coming cross is faced as triumph rather than defeat
explanation: Jesus closes the chapter with courage because his victory over the world is already assured (John 16:33).
